preFix.title: Wet-felted baby shoes
preFix.subtitle: A pair of simple resist-felted shoes for 0-3 months, worked in 100% merino roving
preFix.excerpt: A pair of small resist-felted shoes shaped over a card template. Merino roving is laid in cross-hatch layers around the resist, wet-felted until firm, then the resist is removed and the opening trimmed. Finished sole length approximately 9 cm.
preFix.sourceNotes: Resist-felted baby shoe construction is a standard project in introductory wet-felting workshops in the UK and Europe. Technique synthesised from general wet-felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: These shoes are worked over a card
resist
cut to the shape of a shoe last.
Roving
is laid over the resist in
cross-hatch layers
, wet down with warm soapy water, and rubbed until the fibres hold together as a pre-felt. The resist is then removed, the opening is tidied with scissors, and the shoes go back for
fulling

until firm enough to hold a foot. Finished sole length is approximately 9 cm, which fits 0-3 months. 100% merino roving is used throughout: it felts reliably, is gentle next to a newborn’s skin, and washes at 30°C on a wool cycle.

items.name: Merino roving, 21 micron, in your chosen colour
Card resist, cut to shoe-last shape: sole 10 cm long, 4.5 cm wide at widest point

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the resists

Draw a shoe-last outline on card: 10 cm long, 4.5 cm wide at the ball of the foot, narrowing to 3 cm at the heel. Round off the toe. Cut two identical pieces. These are the resists; each will become the interior of one shoe. The finished sole will measure approximately 9 cm after shrinkage.
Step 2: Lay the first roving layers

Place one card resist on the felting mat. Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them horizontally across the resist, slightly overlapping each wisp, covering the card entirely including a 2 cm border beyond each edge (this border folds over to become the upper). Add a second layer with wisps running vertically. Add a third layer horizontal and a fourth layer vertical. Four layers total on this face.
Step 3: Wet down and rub

Mix a few drops of olive-oil soap into warm water (around 40°C). Drizzle the soapy water over the roving layers until they are saturated but not pooling. Press down gently with a flat hand to push out air. Then rub in small circles with light pressure for two minutes. The fibres should begin to hold together.
Step 4: Fold and cover the resist

Fold the 2 cm border of roving over the card edges onto the other side. Lay four more cross-hatch layers of roving on the back face of the resist, again with a 2 cm overlap at the edge. Wet and rub as before. The entire resist is now enclosed in roving.
Step 5: Roll in the bamboo mat

Place the wrapped resist on the bamboo rolling mat with a piece of bubble wrap or netting on top. Roll the mat firmly around the piece and roll back and forth along the length for 10 minutes. Unroll, turn the piece 90 degrees, re-roll, and roll for another 10 minutes. Repeat until the roving no longer shifts when you pinch and tug it lightly. This is the pre-felt stage.
Step 6: Remove the resist and cut the opening

Once the fibres hold together firmly enough that the piece does not fall apart when handled, slide the card resist out. Use scissors to cut a small oval opening at the top of the shoe, centred over where the foot would enter. Trim neatly. Do not cut into the sole.
Step 7: Full to finished density

Return the open shoe to the soapy water. Scrunch and rub firmly inside and out. Throw the shoe onto the felting mat ten times to agitate the fibres. Roll again in the bamboo mat for 10 more minutes. Check the thickness: the felt should be firm to the touch and not compress noticeably when squeezed. If it still feels soft, continue rubbing and throwing in 5-minute increments.
Step 8: Rinse and shape

Rinse the shoes in water the same temperature as the wash water (30°C) to avoid thermal shock. Squeeze out excess water without wringing. Stuff each shoe firmly with a rolled towel or crumpled paper to hold a three-dimensional shape as it dries. Leave to dry at room temperature, not near direct heat. Once dry, the shoes will hold their shape without stuffing.

items.fix: Add more soapy water and rub in much smaller, lighter circles for the first few minutes until the fibres begin to bond. Only increase pressure once the surface has a slight skin.
items.cause: The fibres were not saturated enough before rubbing began, or rubbing pressure was too heavy too soon
items.problem: The roving shifts and slides off the resist during rubbing

items.fix: Flex the piece gently in all directions to loosen the card, then work it out slowly. Wet the piece again if needed to soften the felt slightly.
items.cause: Felting has progressed further than intended before the resist was removed, causing the felt to grip the card
items.problem: The resist is stuck and will not slide out

items.fix: Return the shoe to hot soapy water and continue scrubbing and throwing for at least 10 more minutes, checking density every 5 minutes.
items.cause: Not enough fulling was done after the resist was removed
items.problem: The finished shoe is floppy and does not hold its shape

items.fix: Weigh roving for each shoe before starting. If one has already shrunk more, full the larger one more aggressively to bring it closer.
items.cause: Uneven roving weight applied to each resist, or inconsistent rolling time
items.problem: The two shoes are different sizes

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a drop of wool wash. Do not wring. Press flat or stuff with paper towel to dry. Do not tumble dry: heat causes further shrinkage. The shoes can be decorated with embroidery or needle-felted dots once dry. Do not add buttons or beads that could detach and pose a choking risk for infants.
